title: Shiny's `startApp()` prints "Listening on …" before binding the listening socket
rationale: Facts: Reporter nbenn (Nicolas Bennett) demonstrates with a trace-based reproducer that shiny's startApp() emits the 'Listening on http://...' message one statement before the startServer() call that binds the socket (R/server.R#L460-L466). The port is already chosen (free-port probe ran earlier) but not yet accepting connections. On a loaded host the window between the two statements causes the URL to refuse connections. Reporter confirms with a socketConnection probe inside a trace() call. Classification hypothesis: Ordering bug — 'Listening on' is not a reliable readiness signal. Duplicate search: No prior shiny issue found for this ordering bug; rstudio/shinytest2#448 is the downstream manifestation, not a duplicate. Wrong-location check: Fix belongs in shiny's server.R; correctly filed here. Regression evidence: Not a regression — this appears to be a long-standing ordering; no older-vs-newer version comparison provided. Reproduction plan: trace('startServer', ..., tracer=quote(socketConnection probe)) as shown in the issue body. Impact: Intermittent CI failures on loaded runners; process supervisors and log watchers parsing 'Listening on' are exposed to the same race. Workaround: move message after startServer() returns (httpuv binds synchronously). Priority: Medium — valid, well-documented bug with identified fix; not production-breaking but has real CI downstream impact (shinytest2#448). Recommended next action: Apply Priority: Medium; a PR moving the message after startServer() would be a clean fix.

title: Consider moving from `selectize.js` to `tom-select`
rationale: Facts: Reporter gadenbuie (Garrick Aden-Buie, Posit team) links to tom-select (https://tom-select.js.org/) and describes it as a modern jQuery-free fork of selectize.js. Created December 2025, updated June 2026. Classification hypothesis: Feature request / library modernization — duplicate of an existing issue. Duplicate search: rstudio/shiny#4227 'Consider implementing an alternative to selectize.js' (created June 2025, open) covers identical territory; its body explicitly mentions Tom Select as one candidate replacement. Issue #4330 is a narrower proposal for the same migration and was filed 6 months after #4227. Wrong-location check: shiny owns the selectize.js dependency; correctly filed here. Regression evidence: N/A. Reproduction plan: N/A — feature request. Impact: Both issues address the same underlying concern; #4227 is the canonical tracking issue. Priority: Low — exploration-level library modernization; no current user-facing breakage. Recommended next action: Label duplicate of #4227, Priority: Low; close or link as a follow-up to #4227.

title: Support dynamically adding children to `layout_column_wrap()`
rationale: Facts: Reporter gadenbuie (Garrick Aden-Buie, Posit team) describes the difficulty of dynamically adding children to layout_column_wrap() using insertUI(): each child is wrapped in fill-carrying div containers, so a naive insertUI() call omits those wrappers. Suggests layout_column_wrap_item() or a more generic item_wrapped() function; notes that as_fill()/as_fillable()/as_fill_carrier() exist but each adds nesting. Issue filed 2023-05-02, follow-up to #559, surfaced for triage 2026-06-11 due to recent activity. Classification hypothesis: Feature request / API gap — no first-class wrapper helper for insertUI() use with layout_column_wrap(). Duplicate search: No duplicate found among open bslib issues. Related: #559 (parent issue), but not a duplicate. Wrong-location check: bslib owns layout_column_wrap() and fill utilities; correctly filed here. Regression evidence: Not a regression — this API gap has existed since layout_column_wrap() was introduced. Reproduction plan: Create a layout_column_wrap() container and call insertUI() to add a new item; observe missing fill-carrier wrappers. Impact: Affects apps with dynamically growing column layouts; workaround available via as_fillable(div(as_fill_carrier(div(...)))) as shown in the issue. Priority: Low — long-standing feature request (3 years) with a documented workaround; does not affect core functionality. Recommended next action: Label Priority: Low; consider for a future htmltools/bslib fill API polish milestone.

title: Intermittent "did not become stable" at init: `AppDriver$new()` navigates before the app's port is bound
rationale: Facts: Reporter nbenn (Nicolas Bennett) provides a complete root-cause analysis: shinytest2 uses the 'Listening on' stderr line as a readiness signal (app-driver-start.R#L243-L245), but per rstudio/shiny#4400 this line is emitted before the socket is bound. On a loaded runner the browser navigates to the not-yet-bound port, lands on a network-error page, and when the app then binds the browser auto-recovers — but the shiny-tracer.js was injected into the error-page context, which is then destroyed. Reporter also identifies a secondary bug: chromote_wait_for_condition() (chromote-methods.R#L138-L140) only checks exceptionDetails, so the -32000 'context destroyed' CDP error returned in results is misread as a successful readiness check, allowing initialization to proceed to the idle check without the tracer present, which then throws 'reading log' and surfaces as 'did not become stable'. Deterministic reproducer provided using trace() + Sys.sleep(5). Full CDP event log with millisecond timestamps attached. Classification hypothesis: Timing-dependent bug in initialization sequencing; secondary bug in CDP error classification. Duplicate search: shinytest2#294 ('AppDriver returns error but app seems to be alive') reports the ready check failing after a timeout, not a navigation-timing race, and the environment uses a separate callr process with manual port polling — different scenario, not a duplicate. rstudio/shiny#4400 is the root cause in shiny, not a duplicate. Wrong-location check: The fix (poll port reachability before navigation) belongs in shinytest2; correctly filed here. Regression evidence: Not a version regression — this is a pre-existing race condition that becomes reliably reproducible under load. Reproduction plan: Provided in issue body (trace() + Sys.sleep(5) in app.R, AppDriver$new() with load_timeout=20000). Impact: Intermittent CI failures on loaded runners for any test suite that uses AppDriver$new(); the testing framework's core initialization path is affected. The secondary -32000 misclassification also masks real context-destruction errors more broadly. Priority: High — well-documented, deterministically reproducible with CDP evidence; affects the reliability of the testing framework's primary initialization path; proposed fix is concrete (poll pingr::is_up before navigate). Recommended next action: Apply Priority: High; accept offered PR to poll port reachability; separately address -32000 error classification in chromote_wait_for_condition().

title: Suggestion: Make ShinyChat compatible with ui.toolbar
rationale: Facts: Reporter slupczynskim (M Slupczynski) discovered that py-shiny 1.6.0's ui.toolbar (introduced in PR #2155) cannot cleanly host a shinychat UI because shinychat always renders an input element, making it redundant alongside a toolbar's own input controls. Two solutions proposed: (a) a parameter to suppress the input element, or (b) a toolbar-aware variant of ui.chat. Filed 2026-06-10. Classification hypothesis: Feature request — compatibility enhancement between shinychat chat UI and py-shiny toolbar component. Duplicate search: No prior shinychat issue for toolbar compatibility found. shinychat#112 (closed) covers markdown rendering, unrelated. Wrong-location check: The fix (e.g., a no-input rendering mode) would live in shinychat's chat UI component; correctly filed here. Could also be tracked as a py-shiny issue for ui.toolbar to accept a slot without a default input, but shinychat is the right primary home. Regression evidence: Not a regression — ui.toolbar is a new feature in py-shiny 1.6.0; no prior behavior to regress from. Reproduction plan: Build a py-shiny app combining ui.toolbar with chat_ui(); observe that the chat input appears redundant alongside toolbar controls. Impact: Affects users building custom chatbot UIs with per-user avatars or rich toolbar layouts; workaround likely possible via CSS/layout composition. Niche use case. Priority: Low — enhancement request for a specific integration pattern; no current users are broken. Recommended next action: Label Priority: Low; consider a chat_ui(input=FALSE) parameter or a toolbar_slot design in a future release.
